TextENGNew: Summer, 1985
changeReason: Japanese 盛夏 means 'midsummer/height of summer'; the change from 'Midsummer' to 'Summer' loses the seasonal specificity.

TextENGNew: I had misjudged the timing and arrived far too early.
The departure board clattered and clicked as it turned over.
changeReason: Japanese 時間表 in an airport context is more specifically a 'departure board' than a generic 'schedule board', changing the visual setting detail.

TextENGNew: My flight to Sapporo still had a long way to go before departure.
There was still well over an hour before boarding began.
...Well then, I might as well close my eyes on one of those benches over there.
I had come straight from the end of my shift, so the fatigue was making me drowsy.
Finding a suitable bench, I set my luggage on the seat beside me and settled in deeply.
...My daughter Miyuki was gazing curiously at the souvenirs and toys displayed in the nearby shop.
I had just asked her if she wanted anything, and she'd said she was fine. Apparently, just looking was enough to keep her entertained.
"...Haaaaaahh..."
A sigh that was undeniably middle-aged escaped from me without any prompting.
I kept thinking of myself as still young, but the truth was I had become quite thoroughly middle-aged.
...The rumour that your stamina declines steadily once you reach your late twenties appeared to be no lie.
Lately I had been completely buried in work and barely had any time to call my own.
Enjoying a solo journey like this might have been the first time since my student days.
Just to be safe, I took out the airline ticket I had slipped into my inner pocket and checked it.
...The purpose of this trip was a reunion with an old friend.
Though calling him an old friend might be stretching it. ...Perhaps "old scoundrel" was more fitting.
changeReason: Japanese 悪友 translated as 'bad friend' vs 'old scoundrel'; the new carries a stronger connotation that alters perception of the relationship.

TextENGNew: After retiring a couple of years ago, he had moved to Sapporo, his mother's hometown.
His mother had passed away not long after, and by all accounts he was now peacefully living out his second chapter in life.
His name was Kuraudo Ooishi.
A retired detective from the Criminal Investigation Division of the Okinomiya Station under a certain prefectural police department.
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Primary Investigation Unit' to 'Criminal Investigation Division' (刑事課), which is a more standard translation of the Japanese term.

TextENGNew: In truth, the time we had spent in direct contact was no more than three days.
After that we had kept in touch through New Year's cards and the like, but we had never met again.
So this reunion would be... our first in seven years.
Which meant we had met seven years ago.
There was something about those days that I absolutely had to ask him about—had to speak with him about.
To explain it properly... I would have to look back on that incident from 1978.
...That kidnapping—the one where everything, from beginning to end, was decided in darkness and buried in darkness.
And... I would have to remember a certain mysterious girl as well.
My name is Mamoru Akasaka.
It was a year that was strangely hot even though it was only June...
Early Summer, 1978
"Goodbye, sensei!!"
"Yes, goodbye. Watch out for cars. Don't stop by a friend's house on the way. And make sure your textbooks for tomorrow are ready before you go to sleep."
Clatter, clatter, clatter!!
The lively sound of children charging out into the hallway.
"But come on—didn't I tell you?!"
"I already have one of those!! I don't need it!!"
The high-pitched voices only children have.
Even the raucous chorus of cicadas seemed to go completely unheard by their ears.
At intersections throughout the residential streets, the children walking home together in their groups parted ways one by one.
Even if a group was still several strong when they left the school gates, the numbers dwindled steadily as they drew closer to their homes.
...So the shrinking number of friends walking beside you was proof of how close to home you were.
"See ya!! Bye!"
"Yeah, see you tomorrow."
He parted from his last friend.
...Around a bend in the lane, a van was parked.
A window was open, the sound of the car radio leaking out. ...It sounded like the news or something.
"...for these reasons, three individuals have been arrested on charges of traffic violations and obstruction of justice."

"The series of protests opposing the Hinamizawa Dam Project has only grown more extreme since last month's violent clash with riot police, and authorities are now on alert for further incidents from radical activists."
"And just last week, there was a direct confrontation with Minister Inukai of Construction at the entrance to the Ministry building..."
At the name "Minister Inukai of Construction," the boy's ears pricked up.
At that moment, a man's face appeared suddenly at the window of the van from which the radio had been playing.
The way he moved when he opened the car door was slightly different from someone simply checking for oncoming traffic.
...Without warning the boy locked eyes with this unfamiliar man, and he flinched...
"...Nightingale, clear."
"Skylark, clear. The two blocks ahead and behind are secured. ...We're good to go!"
The man in the passenger seat murmured quietly.
The man nodded, opened the door, ...and planted himself in front of the boy.
"...Hey. ...Are you Toshiki Inukai?"
The man asked the question while peering at the boy's name tag.
...'inukai toshiki'... that was what was written there in hiragana...
changeReason: Name tag rendering changed: old writes 'Toshiki Inukai' in English order; new preserves Japanese order 'inukai toshiki' and notes it's in hiragana, matching the JPN 平仮名 detail.

TextENGNew: The phone had rung for the third time.
If he reached out, he could easily pick up the receiver.
...But he had a habit of worrying that if he picked up before the first ring had even finished, it would make him look cheap to whoever was calling.
And so he had always made a practice of never picking up the phone until the third ring.
Even he thought it was a pointless habit.
...But there was also a part of him that wondered whether three rings was really even enough, whether he should perhaps make callers wait for five.
His mind full of such trivial thoughts, he savoured the dying echo of the third ring before finally picking up the receiver...
He knew that sometimes, due to a fault in the exchange, calls failed to connect properly.
When that happened, the sensible thing was to hang up and let the other party call again.
...He thought that, and was about to replace the receiver. ...But then he sensed that the line was indeed connected, that there was someone on the other end keeping very quiet...
There was no way they hadn't heard him. ...What on earth was this?
It wasn't that he didn't know about silent calls.
He had simply never received one himself until now.
...Whether that was mere good fortune or not, he found it hard to believe that such a call would ever reach this phone.
This line had no direct incoming calls.
All calls, incoming or outgoing, were routed through an operator.
There was therefore absolutely no way a call from an unidentified party would be put through.
And precisely because of that, he was growing confused, unsure how to handle this incomprehensible silent call...
"...I don't know who you are, but if you have nothing to say, I'm going to hang up. Please try calling again."
He meant those as a parting shot and was about to slam the receiver down.
...It was then that the other party finally spoke.
"...You didn't tell me your name, so I was at a loss. If you're not Minister Inukai, please hang up now."
He couldn't help but be startled by the strangely distorted voice coming from the receiver.
...Was there really a person alive who could speak in such a hoarse, metallic tone?
...No. ...This was... a voice he had heard somewhere before.
Yes—that voice you hear on trashy documentary programmes, when they want to hide someone's identity: "to protect this person's privacy, their voice has been altered." That kind of voice.
...The voice coming through the receiver sounded exactly like that.
"...Who... are you?"
"...That's precisely what I'm trying to ascertain. Are you Minister Inukai?"
He—Inukai—hesitated to answer.
Unable to read the intent behind this suspicious call, he felt a vague and unsettling chill.
He thought about hanging up and asking the operator who had put this through.
...But he checked that impulse with reason and chose, for the time being, to give his name and listen.
"...That's right. This is Inukai. ...I've given you my name, so now you give me yours."
"...My name is of no concern. First, let's start by recognising the situation you currently find yourself in."
"...You're sitting in your seat, talking on the phone, are you not? Then please, try opening the lowest drawer on your right side."
The lowest drawer to the right of the Minister's chair.
...It was a locked drawer—not quite a safe, but one used to store things of a certain importance.
Inukai therefore assumed the caller wanted some important information stored inside it.
"...I'm sorry, but I have no intention of opening that. I have no reason to comply with orders from someone who won't even give me their name."
"If you won't open the drawer, I'll hang up. ...I'm sure you'll regret it. Wouldn't it be better to open it first, and think afterwards?"
Inukai found it deeply unpleasant to comply with this suspicious person's demands.
...However, the word "regret" nagged at him, and he decided to open the drawer.
He took the small key he kept in his wallet and unlocked it.
...His hand stopped just before he pulled it open.
What if there was a bomb inside... and opening the drawer would set it off...?
That delusion had seized hold of him.
Even so, he steeled himself and... pulled it open.
"...Have you opened it? Do you see what's in there?"
"...This is... What is the meaning of this?! Hey!!"
"There's no need for me to explain, is there? I'll give you a little time—please take a moment to think. I'll be in touch again shortly. Now then, if you'll excuse me..."
"Hey! Wait!! Hello!? Hello!?!?"
The call had already ended.
No matter how much he shouted, it would make no difference.
...And yet Inukai, for some time after, apparently couldn't remember that, and went on shouting into the receiver.
...The drawer sat open.
...On top of the many folders packed tightly inside, it rested there, quietly.
``Toshiki Inukai``
changeReason: Name tag rendering changed from English-order romanization to Japanese-order hiragana representation, preserving the original's emphasis on the childlike hiragana writing.

TextENGNew: A schoolboy's name tag, written in hiragana... resting there quietly.
Enshrined in that utterly wrong place, as though it had drawn into itself in fear...
...Do you remember, Yukie? This photograph.
This is where I proposed to you for the first time.

...The brief moment before you nodded back to me—
you couldn't possibly know how taut it felt,
how it stretched out into what seemed like eternity.

My goodness.
To think that was long enough to feel like an eternity...

Compared to all the time I had waited, longing for you to say those words...
it was nothing at all.
Ha ha ha ha ha...
From the day I met you until today...
...Compared to all the time I've lived up till now, it may only be a brief span...

But it has been precious and beautiful, days worthy of being called an eternity—
...days that will never fade.
...And that time continues.
...From here on.

I wonder if the baby will be a boy...
or a girl.
...Either is fine.
Boy or girl... it is the living proof that we truly love each other.
If it's a boy, we'll put one character from your name—'Mamoru'—into his.
If it's a girl, we'll put one from mine—'Yuki'—into hers...
We've had so much fun thinking of names that somehow we still haven't settled on one.

We still have time to deliberate.
...Though not all that much time, really.

...Actually, Yukie.
There's something I need to apologise to you about today.
...It's about your work.
......I'm sorry.

...It seems a troublesome job has come up.
...In the worst case, ...I may not be able to make it... when the baby is born.
It's all right.
You do very important work.
What you do protects the way we live.
...Compared to you, it's nothing.
Please, go.
When you return, I'll be waiting here with the baby.

Thank you.
...And I'm sorry.
...Please don't apologise.
If you truly feel guilty... then someday you can make it up to me.
If I made it up to you as many times as you mean, no amount of money would ever be enough.

Hee hee...
I'm joking.
...Now... off you go...
As I stepped out of the room, I ran into an elderly man.

...Yukie's father.
...My father-in-law.
"...Father."

"I had no intention of intruding.
So I waited in the hall."

"I wasn't trying to eavesdrop, but I couldn't help overhearing.
...Go.
Yukie is that kind of girl.
...No matter what happens, she would never want to be a shackle around your ankles."

"...I have put Yukie through so many sacrifices already.
To be asking her to sacrifice even now—at something as important in her life as giving birth... I am ashamed."

"...If you truly feel that way, ...finish your work and then give Yukie your time.
...She will be happier with that than with you throwing your duty aside to stay by her side."
"...Thank you."

"I know full well that you do very important work.
Be proud of it.
Yukie is looking forward to seeing you return in triumph..."
I jumped into the taxi that had been waiting out front.
Compared to when I had first arrived, the meter had climbed considerably.
...What Yukie and I had quietly shared together was apparently not all that quiet from an outside perspective.

"Sorry to keep you waiting.
Please take me there."

"...Not at all.
Right then, off we go."
The taxi lurched as it shifted gears and accelerated, quickly swallowing the hospital where my wife lay in a wave of buildings.
...If this turned into a real headache of a case, ...I probably wouldn't be coming back for some time.
Calling in a rookie like me—and one who was off duty at that—meant the section chief had almost certainly recalled the entire division.
Emergency summons of senior veteran staff did happen occasionally.
...A situation that called in every last person was something I had never experienced before.
Whatever was happening, there was no question it was going to be a first for me.

...And right when my wife was about to give birth.
If I started cursing my misfortune there would be no end to it.
Even the row of green ginkgo trees that usually lifted my spirits looked somehow muted today...
Before long... the government building came into view.
I held my breath briefly, ...and let the familiar tension return to my whole body.
...Remember the importance of the work you do. The necessity of it. The difficulty of it.
Compose yourself. Sharpen your mind.
...Right.
The taxi pulled up in front of the building.

Kasumigaseki, Tokyo—
Metropolitan Police Department,
Public Security Bureau
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Public Safety Division' to 'Public Security Bureau' (公安部); these are different standard English renderings of the same institution.

TextENGNew:
"Chief, everyone is assembled.
Akasaka-kun, could you draw the blinds?"
The blinds were closed either when they needed the projector, or when the discussion about to take place contained sensitive content.
The blinds descended with a satisfying swish, stealing the daylight from the room.
...Immediately the room shed its crisp morning atmosphere entirely, leaving only the cold, sterile glow of fluorescent lights.
The supervisor confirmed everyone was present and nodded again to the section chief.
...In the charged silence, the chief rose to his feet with quiet gravity and began.

"Approximately forty-eight hours ago, the grandson of the Minister of Construction—who is also the son of one of his senior officials—is believed to have been kidnapped.
It is believed that the Minister, in order to resolve the matter quietly, has not reported it to the police and intends to comply with the kidnappers' demands."
Apparently, roughly forty-eight hours ago, the Minister of Construction's grandson—also the son of one of his senior officials—had been kidnapped.
The use of "apparently" was because the parties directly involved had not acknowledged it.
"Surveillance of communications at the Minister's residence has confirmed several suspicious calls believed to originate from outside the prefecture."

"The kidnapped child is officially said to be receiving treatment for an illness, but there is no trace of hospital visits, nor any witnesses. Based on this and several other factors, we have concluded that a kidnapping has without question taken place."
...How this incident had come to light was omitted here.

A moment's thought would reveal that before this incident was even discovered,
we had already been routinely monitoring communications at the Minister's residence—a practice we do not call 'wiretapping.'
Naturally, there was no court authorisation for this, and it would be very difficult to explain to the general public...
But as in this case, it is a highly effective means of catching wind of serious crimes before they escalate...
I hope you will understand that detecting incidents before they occur is also part of our work in the Public Security Bureau.
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Public Safety Division/Department' to 'Public Security Division' (公安部).

TextENGNew: "Why is he following their demands instead of contacting the police?"
"When even a Minister can't trust the police, you know the world has gone to the dogs."

"It appears that at the outset of the kidnapping, the group demonstrated to the Minister that they had him under quite thorough surveillance, and showed him evidence of it.
Something significant enough that the Minister capitulated and thought twice about calling the police."
"'We'll know the moment you call the police'—something like that?"
"So there may be a mole close to the Minister."
Several of the senior staff who appeared to have experience with this sort of thing let out quiet sighs...

"While the perpetrators' ultimate goal—whether it lies in their demands or in the incident itself—remains unclear,
in any case, there is an extremely high probability that this will escalate into a threat to national interests."
If it were simply a crime motivated by material gain, things would still be manageable.
...But if this was a crime aimed at shaking the Minister and extracting some kind of political action, the background would become suddenly and enormously complicated.
"Since we have no cooperation from the victim, the content of the threats and the demands are entirely unknown..."

"Given that we believe the level of infiltration in this case to be considerable, the investigation will be classified and confined to this division alone.
Furthermore, this case will become our highest priority from today, and all regular duties are to be suspended in response.
Understood?!"
After that the supervisor took over, setting out the course of action with efficient clarity.
If the incident became public, it could endanger the Minister's political career.

...I will spare you the rest of the domino effect, but ultimately it could lead to a collapse of support for the government,
a dissolution and snap election,
and possibly even an expansion of communist influence.
...In other words, delicacy was required.

"Kawasaki and Saeki: monitor communications at the Minister's residence and the child's home around the clock.
Keep track of the Minister's movements.
Report every development as it arises."

"Remaining personnel: investigate connections to this case through your respective assigned organisations.
Focus intensively on any special lines that have shown suspicious activity recently!
And don't rule out foreign involvement either!"

...I had been through one or two situations that were less than smooth.
But I had never seen things get this frantic.
While I was a rookie, high-level discussions carried on all around me.
...I had no intention of shrinking from the work, but I couldn't hide my bewilderment at this unfamiliar situation.

"...and go to Kakiuchi.
As for Akasaka-kun,"
Hearing my name called, my fading attention snapped back.
"Y-Yes!"

"Akasaka-kun, investigate the environmental groups that have brought petitions to the Minister.
Among them, pay particular attention to the group opposing the Hinamizawa Dam Project, which has been making the papers."
"I doubt a residents' group would be behind this, but we have to eliminate every possibility."

"Understood.
The Hinamizawa Dam opposition, then..."

``It would be best to go to the location directly.
Get information from the local police.
Apparently they are a fairly radical organisation, and the local Public Security people should have them flagged.``
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Public Safety Division' to 'Public Security Bureau' (公安部).

TextENGNew:
"Understood.
I'll head out there."

"...Your wife is expecting soon, isn't she?
I'm sorry to put you through this at a time like this, but I need your help.
We don't have the luxury of time, so we have to go in all at once—boots on the ground."

"Yes, I understand.
...My wife would hate for her circumstances to interfere with my work anyway."

"Sorry, and thank you.
...And Kawasaki-kun, Saeki-kun, for backup support..."
...With my wife's delivery so close at hand, the last thing I wanted was to be sent out on assignment.

Even if work was busy, as long as I stayed in Tokyo I could rush to the hospital at a moment's notice.
...Out on assignment, though... that would be difficult.
Even so, I understood the importance of the work I was doing, and I also understood that this was not a time when I could let personal feelings make me selfish.
I would have to apologise to my wife for having to leave her at such a crucial time.
My wife—Yukie—would forgive me with a smile.
It was simply her nature to hate being anyone's burden.
...Even so, part of me wished she would have held me back. That was just male ego.
I'm sorry, ...Yukie.
You're free to grumble to our child forever that Daddy couldn't come to the hospital when they were born because he was too wrapped up in work...
...The following day I headed to Nagoya by bullet train, then transferred to a local line.
Getting to my destination by road would take several hours.

By air, that would have been enough time to reach somewhere around Hong Kong.
...Hinamizawa was by no means close.
I never rode first class except on business trips, and somehow the seats always felt stiff.
I closed my eyes gently and turned over the documents I had reviewed yesterday in my mind.
The organisation under investigation: the "Onigafuchi Guardians."
A residents' group from the local area, opposed to the Hinamizawa Dam development plan.
The protests locally had grown quite heated and increasingly radical.
Even from what was reported in the papers alone—bloodshed in clashes with riot police, forcible interference with dam construction—the list went on and on.
Innumerable petitions to related government bodies, sit-ins, and direct appeals to officials.
...And as an extension of all that, there had been the direct confrontation with the Minister of Construction the other day.
(This, at the end of the day, was why we were now investigating this organisation.)
...If the land they lived on was going to be submerged, it was not difficult to understand why they would fight with everything they had.
That said... could people like this really be capable of kidnapping the Minister of Construction's grandson to demand the plan be cancelled?
Honestly, I had serious doubts.
This kidnapping was an extremely sophisticated operation, and one complex enough that some political backing was to be expected.
...This was not something a residents' protest group could pull off.
...But as the section chief had said, this investigation was one part of eliminating all possibilities.
While I spent my time taking it easy in the prefectural police's records room, the senior staff back in Tokyo would probably solve the case.
Without any help from me.
...The more I thought about it, the more I regretted having to leave Tokyo right when my wife was about to give birth.

It was work.
...There was nothing to be done.
Ding dong...
The announcement that we were approaching my destination brought my drifting mind back to the present.
Prefectural Public Security Division
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Public Safety Division' to 'Public Security Bureau' (公安部).

TextENGNew: "The Onigafuchi Guardians?"
"Yes, they've been quite the presence in this area."
The prefectural Public Security Division had documents and tea waiting for me when I arrived.
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Public Safety Division' to 'Public Security Bureau' (公安部).

TextENGNew:
"To put it simply, they're a residents' group opposing the dam.
Really, you might as well say that every single person whose land will be submerged has risen up together."

"As their old-fashioned name—'guardians who will defend to the last'—suggests, they are a determined and deeply committed lot.
It would be a mistake to think of them as just another mild-mannered residents' group."
The stack of documents prepared for me in the records room was far from thin.

"At the outset they were quite a run-of-the-mill residents' movement.
But at some point after that incident with the riot police, things began to heat up rapidly.
Now you could say they have grown into a radical force, one that could be classified as violent or something close to it."
A violent organisation generally refers to a group that pursues its ideology by means outside the democratic process.
In most cases such groups are driven by revolutionary ideology.
...Considering that, it was impossible not to be surprised by how extreme a residents' movement could become...
Radical residents' movements did happen from time to time.
But this group... appeared to be something entirely beyond that.
...It seemed I would need to somewhat revise my understanding of what the Onigafuchi Guardians was.
"...What would you say is the extent of what the Guardians would be willing to do to achieve their demand—the cancellation of the dam project?"
The kidnapping of the Minister's grandson was classified information.
...That meant, of course, I couldn't share it with the prefectural Public Security Division either.

"As you know, last week's confrontation with Minister Inukai resulted in arrests.
They are certainly dangerous enough."
...So they could be expected to stop at nothing to force their demands.
I skimmed the list of criminal records related to the Onigafuchi Guardians documented in the materials.
...Every entry was violent in nature, with not the faintest trace of any intention to respect the law.
"Could you give me some specifics?"
The chief opened a manila folder, sorted through the uneven sheets inside, and spread them out on the desk.

"Raids on the construction site have been by far the most frequent.
In the early days there were constant minor offences—cutting power cables, filling locks with gravel, smashing office windows with thrown rocks."
Of course, in the very beginning it was demonstrations, sit-ins, leaflet campaigns—lively but democratic forms of resistance.

But then came the incident where the demonstrators and riot police clashed and turned into a brawl, producing numerous injuries and arrests. It was from that point that the Onigafuchi Guardians began to take on the truly demonic character their name implied...
"If the raids kept coming, wouldn't the local police have stepped up their response?"

"Of course they were on high alert.
But those people are locals, after all.
What can the police do if they slip in under cover of darkness?"
...The entire area of Hinamizawa surrounding the dam site might as well have been enemy territory.
No matter how hard the police tried to secure the perimeter... the locals would slip through with ease.

"After the police tightened security, the radicalisation actually accelerated, as if they were being provoked.
...See here? Can you see how things began to escalate around this point?"

"...An office set ablaze.
...Construction machinery sabotaged.
...Sabotaged?
...Surely not explosives?"

"Nothing like that.
Those people—they pour sugar cubes into the fuel tanks.
Once that's done the engine seizes."
...Even in Japan, I had read that people had done similar things to occupying forces' vehicles right after the war ended.
...Compared to mere mischief like breaking windows, it was extremely violent and aggressive.
"...If things have gotten to that point, the local police have completely lost face."

"The arson was a bit much, yes.
After that the local police dramatically increased their round-the-clock presence.
The raids on the construction site quieted down somewhat after that."
He had used the phrase 'quieted down,' but... that was still only roughly halfway through the list of offences.

"And then those people—
once they found that direct attacks on the site had become difficult, they switched to personal attacks.
...The first targets were the workers at the construction office."
From that point it became a shadow war.
changeReason: Old 'there was silence' misrenders Japanese 暗闘 (secret struggle/shadow war); new 'shadow war' correctly conveys the escalation to covert conflict.

TextENGNew: It was like watching a guerrilla campaign played out in dense jungle...

Harassment, threats, and violence against the construction workers.
Insults hurled at them—and rocks.
"...Despite the considerable number of complaints filed, there seem to be very few arrests."

"Of course not.
For one thing, there are no witnesses.
And on top of that, even if you catch someone red-handed, alibis materialise out of thin air."

"...?
What do you mean by that?"

"Hmmm.
...Take this hypothetical: you're walking through Hinamizawa,
and a certain man stabs you with a knife.
You remember the assailant clearly—you have his name and address."

"However, the knife has no fingerprints on it, and there is no other physical evidence.
...Normally you'd think this was a straightforward assault case, wouldn't you?
In Hinamizawa, it's a perfect crime."
Every single person... the entire village is complicit.
To protect the man who attacked you, the whole village coordinates its stories and provides him with an alibi.
...They will probably even go so far as to fabricate evidence.

"The only recourse is to take them to trial.
But with no physical evidence, and a steady stream of testimony surfacing to cover for the suspect,
...any prosecutor would hesitate to indict."

"Unless it's murder, if it's something like a cut on the forehead from a thrown rock or bruising from a punch, even if the suspect is identified, it almost always gets dropped for insufficient evidence."
Every offence was undoubtedly committed by someone in Hinamizawa... but it was impossible to identify who.
And even if they could identify the perpetrator, the circumstantial evidence necessary to hold them legally was never forthcoming...
The villagers, fully versed in all of this, carried on their insidious, vicious... and relentless campaign of personal attacks...

"The victims can't have been satisfied with that outcome, surely.
Couldn't they have appealed to the Prosecution Review Commission?"

"Well, you see, every member of the Review Commission... doesn't want to get dragged into something so troublesome.
Nobody wants to stick their nose into anything connected to Hinamizawa."
The Prosecution Review Commission consists of local residents selected at random from the relevant district.
changeReason: Institution name changed from 'Inquest Committee' to 'Prosecution Review Commission' (検察審査会); the new is the standard English term for this Japanese legal institution.

TextENGNew: ...They have the authority to refer cases back for re-investigation where the prosecutor has declined to indict.
changeReason: Japanese 不起訴 means 'non-indictment'; old 'fails to get a conviction' implies a trial occurred, while new 'declined to indict' correctly represents the pre-trial stage.

TextENGNew: It is a system designed to inject a measure of public will into the decisions of the legally expert prosecution...
...But the very fact of it being composed of local residents was working against it here.

"They don't want to get involved?
Why is that?"

"...Hmm...
How to put this...
You might say they are afraid...
...That place is a little unusual."

"...Unusual?
Are you suggesting there is some kind of political backing?"

"No... it's a little different from that...
...Well, just think of it as: they're afraid.
It's a little difficult to explain right now.
....Oh, that's it. There's a simpler way to show you."
The chief wiped his forehead with a handkerchief and began opening a file labelled 'List of Onigafuchi Guardians Members.'

"The power of the Onigafuchi Guardians, you see...
...is that their ranks include many individuals with considerable influence over the surrounding towns.
Could you take a look at this?"
...I looked. What I saw surprised me a little.
Prefectural and municipal assembly members. Officers of the Chamber of Commerce. Executives of retailers' associations.
...Executives of neighbourhood associations and PTA liaisons... there were more than a few people with significant influence both locally and in the surrounding area.

"Around here, it's standard practice to observe neutrality towards Hinamizawa's dam protests.
...If you were to say anything in support of the dam... you'd never know how it might come back to hurt you."
"After all, the levers of power in every town around here are held by people from Hinamizawa."

``The list of Prosecution Review Commission members is undisclosed, isn't it?
Surely there must be some measures in place to protect them from retaliation?``
changeReason: Institution name changed from 'Inquisition Committee' to 'Prosecution Review Commission' (検察審査会); the new is a more accurate rendering.

TextENGNew: ``...Well, it is undisclosed, and their privacy is nominally protected.@k@vS05/00/104300058. But the person who manages that is from the local area.``
changeReason: Old specifies 'from the Okinomiya Municipal Office' while JPN says 地元の人間 (a local person) — fabricated institutional detail, though the core meaning (managed by someone local) is preserved.

TextENGNew:
"Even with a duty of confidentiality, ...you never know how people are connected.
The people of Hinamizawa have exceptionally tight bonds of that kind."
...When you consider the web of community obligations, duty, and local ties, the resulting information network is not to be underestimated.
It is by no means unusual for the housewives in a neighbourhood to know which child from which house is in which year and class, what subjects they're good at, what vegetables they hate.

"...And you see, there is a yakuza organisation here with deep ties to Hinamizawa.
It appears they are providing full backing to this current unrest.
They are, it seems, the most potent factor."

"Yakuza?
Siding with the residents against the dam?
...It's hard to see where their interests align."

"It's not complicated at all.@k@vS05/00/104300066. Actually, you see, the head of that yakuza organisation is originally from Hinamizawa.@k@vS05/00/104300067. He was adopted into the family of one of the village's leading figures."
changeReason: Japanese 組長 means 'boss/head'; old 'lieutenant' vs new 'head' represent very different ranks in the yakuza hierarchy.

TextENGNew: ...What exactly was this village of Hinamizawa?
I had assumed it was some remote and run-down rural hamlet.

And yet for some reason it wielded considerable influence over the surrounding area and possessed a powerful sense of solidarity.
They would use any violent means necessary to protect their village.

The chief had put it as people being afraid because there were so many influential individuals there,
but somehow the impression I was getting was that it was the village itself they were afraid of.
...There was clearly something different here from what I had read about in Tokyo—a simple residents' movement opposing a dam.
For some reason, a feeling of unease had taken root.
...I was going to ask my question in the worst possible way.
changeReason: Japanese 最低の部類 means 'the worst/lowest category'; old 'most basic' vs new 'worst possible way' convey very different self-assessments.

TextENGNew: ...In other words, I was going to come straight out with what I wanted to know.

"...Chief.
...This is purely hypothetical, but..."
"Yes?"

"...The Onigafuchi Guardians.
In order to halt the dam project...
...Do you think they could, hypothetically, resort to threatening someone of importance?"
The chief answered immediately.

"It is possible.
In fact, they have already turned up at municipal offices, the prefectural government, and local Ministry of Construction offices and done things that amount to intimidation.
Several construction workers' family members have also reported being followed by suspicious individuals."
...Well, one might expect that much.
To wage their campaign against the dam, they had raided the construction site, destroyed heavy machinery, and burned the office down.
...People with no qualms about attacking construction workers and visiting threats and violence upon them... would hardly hesitate to go that far.
...But... that was all.
Threats and violence were within reach of anyone.
But the kidnapping of the Minister's grandson... was a crime of an entirely different order.
Not only the sheer difficulty of pulling off the kidnapping itself, but the skill to bring the Minister to his knees the moment negotiations began—this was clearly not the work of amateurs.
...Did these people have the capability to carry out a crime of this magnitude...?
That was the crux of the matter.
...Was the Onigafuchi Guardians an organisation capable of this incident?
...To ask that question, I needed to go again.
...I asked in the worst possible way.
changeReason: Japanese 最低の部類 means 'the worst/lowest category'; old 'most basic' vs new 'worst possible way' convey very different self-assessments.

TextENGNew: "...Again, just hypothetically—do you think they could kidnap a relative of a government official and use them as a threat?"
There's no way they could pull off something that outrageous.
That was the answer I was most hoping for.
If that was the answer... my work was as good as half done.

I might be back in time for my wife's delivery.
...That was how it was supposed to go.
The chief, without any sign of hesitation, replied without a pause:

"They just might.
...There's no telling what they're capable of."
...I'm sorry, Yukie.
...It seemed my work was not going to end so easily after all.

"...Oh, Akasaka-kun.
Good work.
How is the information gathering going at your assigned area?"

``It's coming along.
It seems I'll be able to make contact with the local Public Security people, so I'm planning to head over there.
How is the investigation going in Tokyo?``
changeReason: Department name changed from 'Public Safety Division' to 'Public Security Bureau' (公安部).

TextENGNew:
"The others are making progress too.
But the number of organisations to investigate is astronomical.
Time isn't really something we can afford."
...No new developments at this point.

No matter how suspicious the Onigafuchi Guardians was, if they solved it in Tokyo my work would be done.
...It looked like that wishful thinking would not come true.
I replaced the receiver and let my gaze drift across the valley of unfamiliar buildings visible through the window...

"Akasaka-san.
A car we can lend you has just returned.
I'll take you to it, so please follow me."
"Ah, thank you."
He showed me to the underground car park via the lift, where a sedan battered from years of use had been prepared.
The steering wheel had a slight tendency to drift left, which was a little worrying, but it would serve well enough as my mode of transport for the time being.
My destination: Shishibone City.

...Home territory of the Onigafuchi Guardians.
The Okinomiya Police Station held the front line.
